**Lifetime Value** (LTV) in insurance is the expected net present value of all future profits generated by a customer relationship over the insured's expected tenure with the company, used to inform pricing and acquisition cost decisions for profitable customer segments.

> $$LTV = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{(\text{Premium}_t - \text{Losses}_t - \text{Expenses}_t)}{(1+r)^t} \times p(\text{retain through } t)$$

- High acquisition costs (agent commissions, marketing, underwriting) may justify a first-year rate that is below full cost if future policy years generate sufficient profit to compensate
- Renewal rates, loss experience improvement over time (seasoning effect), and cross-sell opportunities all increase LTV and strengthen the case for competitive new-business pricing
- LTV analysis must be conducted carefully to avoid adverse selection: below-cost pricing to attract a segment may attract the wrong mix if the assumed retention or loss improvement does not materialize
- Actuarial rate adequacy is assessed on a policy-period basis; LTV considerations are a business decision layer applied on top of the actuarial indicated rate

> [!example]- LTV Justifying Below-Cost First-Year Rate {Example}
> An insurer acquires a homeowner customer for $200 acquisition cost. Year 1 underwriting profit is −$50. Expected renewal profit is $120/year for 4 additional years. Discount rate is 8%.
>
> > [!answer]-
> > $$LTV = -\$200 - \$50 + \frac{\$120}{1.08} + \frac{\$120}{1.08^2} + \frac{\$120}{1.08^3} + \frac{\$120}{1.08^4} \approx -\$250 + \$397 = +\$147$$
> > The positive LTV justifies accepting the first-year loss; the relationship is profitable over its expected life.
